title: The Man Who Counts Steps
text_zh: 我家楼下住着一个退休的老工程师，每天早晨六点准时出门散步。邻居们都知道他有个奇怪的习惯——他会数自己走过的每一级台阶。不是默默地数，而是嘴唇微微动着，偶尔能听到极轻的声音。有人问过他为什么这样做，他说：「年轻的时候设计桥梁，每一个数据都关乎安全，数字就成了我思考的方式。」他说退休以后，世界突然变得模糊了，不再有图纸，不再有需要他负责的结构。数台阶让他重新感到踏实，仿佛每一步都有确切的意义。我注意到，他数台阶的时候表情是平静的，甚至带着一种隐约的满足。也许我们每个人都在用自己的方式给混乱的日常生活施加某种秩序。他的秩序碰巧是算术，而我的秩序也许是在每天同一时间泡同一种茶。区别不大，本质相同——我们都害怕失去对世界的把握。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóu xià zhù zhe yí gè tuìxiū de lǎo gōngchéngshī, měi tiān zǎochen liù diǎn zhǔnshí chūmén sànbù. Línjū men dōu zhīdào tā yǒu gè qíguài de xíguàn——tā huì shǔ zìjǐ zǒuguò de měi yí jí táijiē. Bú shì mòmò de shǔ, ér shì zuǐchún wēiwēi dòng zhe, ǒu'ěr néng tīng dào jí qīng de shēngyīn. Yǒu rén wènguò tā wèishénme zhèyàng zuò, tā shuō: 「Niánqīng de shíhou shèjì qiáoliáng, měi yí gè shùjù dōu guānhū ānquán, shùzì jiù chéng le wǒ sīkǎo de fāngshì.」Tā shuō tuìxiū yǐhòu, shìjiè tūrán biàn de móhu le, bú zài yǒu túzhǐ, bú zài yǒu xūyào tā fùzé de jiégòu. Shǔ táijiē ràng tā chóngxīn gǎndào tāshi, fǎngfú měi yí bù dōu yǒu quèqiè de yìyì. Wǒ zhùyì dào, tā shǔ táijiē de shíhou biǎoqíng shì píngjìng de, shènzhì dài zhe yì zhǒng yǐnyuē de mǎnzú. Yěxǔ wǒmen měi gè rén dōu zài yòng zìjǐ de fāngshì gěi hùnluàn de rìcháng shēnghuó shījiā mǒu zhǒng zhìxù. Tā de zhìxù pèngqiǎo shì suànshù, ér wǒ de zhìxù yěxǔ shì zài měi tiān tóng yí shíjiān pào tóng yì zhǒng chá. Qūbié bú dà, běnzhì xiāngtóng——wǒmen dōu hàipà shīqù duì shìjiè de bǎwò.
text_en: A retired old engineer lives on the floor below me and goes for a walk at exactly six every morning. The neighbors all know about his strange habit—he counts every single step he climbs. Not silently, but with his lips moving slightly; occasionally you can catch an extremely faint sound. Someone once asked him why he does this, and he said: 'When I was young I designed bridges, every piece of data was a matter of safety, and numbers became my way of thinking.' He said that after retirement, the world suddenly became blurry—no more blueprints, no more structures he was responsible for. Counting steps made him feel grounded again, as if each step had a definite meaning. I've noticed that when he counts steps, his expression is calm, even carrying a faint satisfaction. Perhaps each of us is using our own method to impose some kind of order on the chaos of daily life. His order happens to be arithmetic, and mine is perhaps brewing the same tea at the same time every day. The difference is small, the essence the same—we're all afraid of losing our grip on the world.

questions.q_en: Why does the old engineer count steps?

title: The Calligrapher's Window
text_zh: 街角那家书法培训班的窗户永远开着，哪怕冬天也只关一半。路过的时候能看到一位五十多岁的女老师站在长桌前，手臂悬空，毛笔缓慢而果断地落下。她很少说话，学生们也很安静，整个房间只有墨在纸上移动的沙沙声。我曾经站在窗外看了将近二十分钟。她写的是草书，笔画之间的连接流畅得像水流过石头。偶尔她会停下来，端详自己写的字，然后轻轻叹一口气——不是失望，更像是对某种无法完全捕捉的美的惋惜。我后来查过，草书的精髓在于「意在笔先」，落笔之前整个字的走势就应该在脑中完成。这让我想到，也许沉默的人并非无话可说，而是他们的表达方式需要更长的酝酿期。那位老师的沉默不是空洞的，是饱满的——每一笔都是她斟酌了许久的句子。
text_pinyin: Jiējiǎo nà jiā shūfǎ péixùn bān de chuānghu yǒngyuǎn kāi zhe, nǎpà dōngtiān yě zhǐ guān yí bàn. Lùguò de shíhou néng kàn dào yí wèi wǔshí duō suì de nǚ lǎoshī zhàn zài cháng zhuō qián, shǒubì xuánkōng, máobǐ huǎnmàn ér guǒduàn de luòxià. Tā hěn shǎo shuōhuà, xuéshengmen yě hěn ānjìng, zhěnggè fángjiān zhǐyǒu mò zài zhǐ shàng yídòng de shāshā shēng. Wǒ céngjīng zhàn zài chuāng wài kàn le jiāngjìn èrshí fēnzhōng. Tā xiě de shì cǎoshū, bǐhuà zhījiān de liánjiē liúchàng de xiàng shuǐ liúguò shítou. Ǒu'ěr tā huì tíng xiàlái, duānxiáng zìjǐ xiě de zì, ránhòu qīngqīng tàn yì kǒu qì——bú shì shīwàng, gèng xiàng shì duì mǒu zhǒng wúfǎ wánquán bǔzhuō de měi de wǎnxī. Wǒ hòulái cháguò, cǎoshū de jīngsuǐ zàiyú 「yì zài bǐ xiān」, luòbǐ zhīqián zhěnggè zì de zǒushì jiù yīnggāi zài nǎo zhōng wánchéng. Zhè ràng wǒ xiǎngdào, yěxǔ chénmò de rén bìngfēi wú huà kě shuō, ér shì tāmen de biǎodá fāngshì xūyào gèng cháng de yùnniàng qī. Nà wèi lǎoshī de chénmò bú shì kōngdòng de, shì bǎomǎn de——měi yì bǐ dōu shì tā zhēnzhuó le xǔjiǔ de jùzi.
text_en: The calligraphy school on the street corner always has its window open—even in winter it's only half-closed. Walking by, you can see a female teacher in her fifties standing at a long table, arm suspended in air, the brush descending slowly but decisively. She rarely speaks, the students are also quiet, and the only sound in the whole room is the rustling of ink moving across paper. I once stood outside the window watching for nearly twenty minutes. She was writing cursive script, the connections between strokes flowing like water over stones. Occasionally she would stop, study what she had written, then let out a soft sigh—not disappointment, more like a lament for a beauty that cannot quite be captured. I later looked it up: the essence of cursive script lies in 'the intention preceding the brush'—before the brush touches paper, the entire trajectory of the character should already be complete in the mind. This made me think that perhaps quiet people are not without things to say; rather, their form of expression requires a longer period of gestation. That teacher's silence is not hollow—it is full. Each stroke is a sentence she has deliberated over for a long time.

title: Overnight Rain
text_zh: 昨晚下了一场大雨，我是被雨声吵醒的。躺在床上听了很久，辨别着不同的声音层次：雨打在窗玻璃上是清脆的敲击声，落在空调外机上是沉闷的鼓点，顺着排水管流下去则是持续不断的低语。城市的夜晚本来就不安静，但雨声有一种奇特的效果——它把所有其他的噪音都吸收了，仿佛给整个世界蒙上了一层柔软的布。我想起小时候住在乡下，暴雨天会停电，全家人围坐在蜡烛旁边，谁也不急着说话。那种沉默和现在独自听雨的沉默性质完全不同：前者是被自然包围的安心，后者是成年人深夜独处时那种清醒的孤独。但两种沉默都有各自的价值。清晨起来，空气被洗过一遍，路面反射着天光，一切都显得异常清晰。
text_pinyin: Zuówǎn xià le yì chǎng dà yǔ, wǒ shì bèi yǔshēng chǎoxǐng de. Tǎng zài chuáng shàng tīng le hěn jiǔ, biànbié zhe bùtóng de shēngyīn céngcì: yǔ dǎ zài chuāng bōli shàng shì qīngcuì de qiāojī shēng, luò zài kōngtiáo wàijī shàng shì chénmèn de gǔdiǎn, shùn zhe páishuǐ guǎn liú xiàqù zé shì chíxù búduàn de dīyǔ. Chéngshì de yèwǎn běnlái jiù bù ānjìng, dàn yǔshēng yǒu yì zhǒng qítè de xiàoguǒ——tā bǎ suǒyǒu qítā de zàoyīn dōu xīshōu le, fǎngfú gěi zhěnggè shìjiè méng shàng le yì céng róuruǎn de bù. Wǒ xiǎngqǐ xiǎo shíhou zhù zài xiāngxià, bàoyǔ tiān huì tíngdiàn, quánjiā rén wéizuò zài làzhú pángbiān, shéi yě bù jí zhe shuōhuà. Nà zhǒng chénmò hé xiànzài dúzì tīng yǔ de chénmò xìngzhì wánquán bùtóng: qiánzhě shì bèi zìrán bāowéi de ānxīn, hòuzhě shì chéngnián rén shēnyè dúchǔ shí nà zhǒng qīngxǐng de gūdú. Dàn liǎng zhǒng chénmò dōu yǒu gèzì de jiàzhí. Qīngchén qǐlái, kōngqì bèi xǐguò yí biàn, lùmiàn fǎnshè zhe tiānguāng, yíqiè dōu xiǎnde yìcháng qīngxī. Yěxǔ hùnluàn zhīhòu de qīngxī, cái shì zuì zhíde zhēnxī de nà zhǒng.
text_en: There was a heavy rain last night—I was woken by the sound of it. I lay in bed listening for a long time, distinguishing the different layers of sound: rain hitting the window glass was a crisp tapping; landing on the outdoor AC unit, a muffled drumbeat; flowing down the drainpipe, a continuous murmur. City nights are never quiet to begin with, but rain has a peculiar effect—it absorbs all other noise, as if draping a soft cloth over the whole world. I remembered living in the countryside as a child, how during rainstorms the power would go out and the whole family would sit around a candle, nobody in a rush to speak. That silence and the silence of listening to rain alone now are entirely different in nature: the former was the peace of being surrounded by nature; the latter is the lucid loneliness of an adult alone in the deep night. But both silences have their own value. Getting up in the morning, the air washed clean, the road reflecting the sky's light, everything appears exceptionally clear.

questions.q_en: What peculiar effect does the author attribute to the sound of rain?

ràng chéngshì biàn de gèng chǎo

title: The Photograph on the Fridge
text_zh: 我家冰箱上用磁铁贴着一张褪色的照片，是我父母年轻时在西湖边拍的。照片里他们穿着八十年代的衣服，我母亲烫着卷发，我父亲戴着一副笨重的眼镜，两个人笑得很自然。这张照片已经在冰箱上贴了至少十五年，久到我几乎不再注意它的存在。直到上个月家里来了客人，对方指着照片问「这是谁」的时候，我才重新认真地看了一遍。照片里的两个人和我现在认识的父母判若两人——不仅仅是外貌上的区别，而是那种毫无防备的笑容。成年以后，我很少在他们脸上看到那样的表情了。我不确定是岁月磨去了那种天真，还是他们只是学会了在孩子面前保持另一种面孔。也许每个父母都有一个「成为父母之前」的版本，而那个版本会慢慢退到家庭相册的深处，偶尔被一个陌生人的提问唤醒。那天晚上，我把照片取下来擦了擦灰尘，又放回了原处。有些东西不需要移动，只需要偶尔被重新看见。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā bīngxiāng shàng yòng cítiě tiē zhe yì zhāng tuìsè de zhàopiàn, shì wǒ fùmǔ niánqīng shí zài Xīhú biān pāi de. Zhàopiàn lǐ tāmen chuān zhe bāshí niándài de yīfu, wǒ mǔqīn tàng zhe juǎnfà, wǒ fùqīn dài zhe yí fù bènzhòng de yǎnjìng, liǎng gè rén xiào de hěn zìrán. Zhè zhāng zhàopiàn yǐjīng zài bīngxiāng shàng tiē le zhìshǎo shíwǔ nián, jiǔ dào wǒ jīhū bú zài zhùyì tā de cúnzài. Zhídào shàng gè yuè jiālǐ lái le kèren, duìfāng zhǐ zhe zhàopiàn wèn 「zhè shì shéi」de shíhou, wǒ cái chóngxīn rènzhēn de kàn le yí biàn. Zhàopiàn lǐ de liǎng gè rén hé wǒ xiànzài rènshi de fùmǔ pànruòliǎngrén——bù jǐnjǐn shì wàimào shàng de qūbié, ér shì nà zhǒng háo wú fángbèi de xiàoróng. Chéngnián yǐhòu, wǒ hěn shǎo zài tāmen liǎn shàng kàndào nàyàng de biǎoqíng le. Wǒ bù quèdìng shì suìyuè mó qù le nà zhǒng tiānzhēn, háishi tāmen zhǐshì xuéhuì le zài háizi miànqián bǎochí lìng yì zhǒng miànkǒng. Yěxǔ měi gè fùmǔ dōu yǒu yí gè 「chéngwéi fùmǔ zhīqián」de bǎnběn, ér nàge bǎnběn huì mànmàn tuì dào jiātíng xiàngcè de shēnchù, ǒu'ěr bèi yí gè mòshēngrén de tíwèn huànxǐng. Nà tiān wǎnshang, wǒ bǎ zhàopiàn qǔ xiàlái cā le cā huīchén, yòu fàng huí le yuánchù. Yǒuxiē dōngxi bù xūyào yídòng, zhǐ xūyào ǒu'ěr bèi chóngxīn kànjiàn.
text_en: On our fridge, held by a magnet, is a faded photograph of my parents taken by West Lake when they were young. In the photo they're wearing eighties clothes—my mother with permed curls, my father in a pair of clunky glasses, both laughing naturally. This photo has been on the fridge for at least fifteen years, so long I barely notice it exists anymore. It wasn't until a guest visited last month and pointed at the photo asking 'Who's this?' that I really looked at it again. The two people in the photo and the parents I know now are like two entirely different people—not just in appearance, but in that completely unguarded smile. Since becoming an adult, I rarely see that expression on their faces. I'm not sure whether time wore away that innocence, or they simply learned to maintain a different face in front of their children. Perhaps every parent has a 'before becoming a parent' version of themselves, and that version slowly retreats to the depths of the family album, occasionally awakened by a stranger's question. That evening I took the photo down, wiped off the dust, and put it back. Some things don't need to be moved—they just need to occasionally be seen again.

title: The Night Shift Convenience Store
text_zh: 凌晨两点的便利店有一种白天完全不具备的气质。灯光依然是惨白的荧光灯，货架上的商品依然整齐地排列着，但整个空间的意义变了。白天它是效率的代名词——人们快速进出，拿了东西就走。到了深夜，它变成了某种庇护所。我常常加班到很晚，回家路上会在楼下的便利店停留十分钟。收银台后面总是同一个年轻人，戴着耳机，面前放着一本翻了一半的书。他从不主动搭话，但偶尔我们会交换一个默契的眼神——那种只有深夜还醒着的人才懂的眼神。我注意到深夜的顾客都有一种共同的特征：动作很慢。没有人赶时间，大家都在货架之间缓缓移动，像是在水里走路。有个穿着护士服的女人几乎每次都会站在杂志架前翻几页，虽然她从来不买。一个戴眼镜的中年男人每次只买一罐啤酒和一包花生。这些人白天可能永远不会相遇，但在凌晨两点的荧光灯下，我们构成了一个沉默的、临时的共同体。谁也不认识谁，但谁都不觉得孤单。
text_pinyin: Língchén liǎng diǎn de biànlì diàn yǒu yì zhǒng báitiān wánquán bù jùbèi de qìzhì. Dēngguāng yīrán shì cǎnbái de yíngguāngdēng, huòjià shàng de shāngpǐn yīrán zhěngqí de páiliè zhe, dàn zhěnggè kōngjiān de yìyì biàn le. Báitiān tā shì xiàolǜ de dàimíngcí——rénmen kuàisù jìnchū, ná le dōngxi jiù zǒu. Dào le shēnyè, tā biànchéng le mǒu zhǒng bìhùsuǒ. Wǒ chángcháng jiābān dào hěn wǎn, huíjiā lùshang huì zài lóu xià de biànlì diàn tíngliú shí fēnzhōng. Shōuyín tái hòumiàn zǒngshì tóng yí gè niánqīng rén, dài zhe ěrjī, miànqián fàng zhe yì běn fān le yí bàn de shū. Tā cóng bù zhǔdòng dā huà, dàn ǒu'ěr wǒmen huì jiāohuàn yí gè mòqì de yǎnshén——nà zhǒng zhǐyǒu shēnyè hái xǐng zhe de rén cái dǒng de yǎnshén. Wǒ zhùyì dào shēnyè de gùkè dōu yǒu yì zhǒng gòngtóng de tèzhēng: dòngzuò hěn màn. Méiyǒu rén gǎn shíjiān, dàjiā dōu zài huòjià zhījiān huǎnhuǎn yídòng, xiàng shì zài shuǐ lǐ zǒulù. Yǒu gè chuān zhe hùshi fú de nǚrén jīhū měi cì dōu huì zhàn zài zázhì jià qián fān jǐ yè, suīrán tā cónglái bù mǎi. Yí gè dài yǎnjìng de zhōngnián nánrén měi cì zhǐ mǎi yì guàn píjiǔ hé yì bāo huāshēng. Zhèxiē rén báitiān kěnéng yǒngyuǎn bú huì xiāngyù, dàn zài língchén liǎng diǎn de yíngguāngdēng xià, wǒmen gòuchéng le yí gè chénmò de, línshí de gòngtóngtǐ. Shéi yě bú rènshi shéi, dàn shéi dōu bù juéde gūdān.
text_en: A convenience store at two in the morning possesses a quality entirely absent during the day. The lighting is still the same pale fluorescent, the products still neatly arranged on shelves, but the meaning of the whole space has changed. During the day it's a synonym for efficiency—people dart in and out, grab what they need and leave. Late at night it becomes a kind of shelter. I often work overtime until very late and stop for ten minutes at the convenience store downstairs on my way home. Behind the register it's always the same young person, wearing headphones, a half-read book open in front of them. He never initiates conversation, but occasionally we exchange a knowing glance—the kind only people still awake in the deep night understand. I've noticed that late-night customers all share a common trait: they move slowly. Nobody is in a rush; everyone drifts between the shelves, as if walking through water. A woman in a nurse's uniform almost always stands by the magazine rack flipping through a few pages, though she never buys one. A bespectacled middle-aged man only ever buys one can of beer and a bag of peanuts. These people might never cross paths during the day, but under the fluorescent lights at 2 AM, we form a silent, temporary community. Nobody knows anyone, but nobody feels alone.

title: The Calligraphy of Rain on Stone
text_zh: 雨天走过老街的时候，我注意到石板路上的水渍形成了一种天然的书法。那些不规则的水迹顺着石板之间的缝隙蔓延，像是某种古老文字的笔画——有的粗犷豪放，有的纤细婉转。每一块石板因为磨损程度不同，吸收雨水的速度也不一样：磨得光滑的石板表面水珠汇聚成一面镜子，倒映着灰色的天空和行人匆忙的身影；磨损严重的石板则迅速吸干了水分，呈现出一种深沉的暗色。这种明暗交替构成了一幅抽象的画卷，只有在特定的光线和湿度下才会呈现。我蹲下来仔细观察，发现石板上还有一些极细的纹路——那是几百年来无数双脚反复踩踏留下的痕迹，是时间刻在石头上的签名。一个老人撑着伞走过，他的布鞋在湿石板上发出轻柔的声响，像是在小心翼翼地朗读这些看不见的文字。雨停以后这些书法就会消失，但石头记得每一次降雨，正如它记得每一个走过的人。
text_pinyin: Yǔ tiān zǒu guò lǎo jiē de shíhou, wǒ zhùyì dào shíbǎn lù shàng de shuǐzì xíngchéng le yì zhǒng tiānrán de shūfǎ. Nàxiē bù guīzé de shuǐjì shùn zhe shíbǎn zhī jiān de fèngxì mànyán, xiàng shì mǒu zhǒng gǔlǎo wénzì de bǐhuà — yǒu de cūguǎng háofàng, yǒu de xiānxì wǎnzhuǎn. Měi yí kuài shíbǎn yīnwèi mósǔn chéngdù bùtóng, xīshōu yǔshuǐ de sùdù yě bù yíyàng: mó de guānghuá de shíbǎn biǎomiàn shuǐzhū huìjù chéng yí miàn jìngzi, dàoyìng zhe huīsè de tiānkōng hé xíngrén cōngmáng de shēnyǐng; mósǔn yánzhòng de shíbǎn zé xùnsù xī gān le shuǐfèn, chéngxiàn chū yì zhǒng shēnchén de ànsè. Zhè zhǒng míng'àn jiāotì gòuchéng le yì fú chōuxiàng de huàjuǎn, zhǐ yǒu zài tèdìng de guāngxiàn hé shīdù xià cái huì chéngxiàn. Wǒ dūn xiàlái zǐxì guānchá, fāxiàn shíbǎn shàng hái yǒu yìxiē jí xì de wénlù — nà shì jǐ bǎi nián lái wúshù shuāng jiǎo fǎnfù cǎità liú xià de hénjì, shì shíjiān kè zài shítou shàng de qiānmíng. Yí gè lǎorén chēng zhe sǎn zǒu guò, tā de bù xié zài shī shíbǎn shàng fāchū qīngróu de shēngxiǎng, xiàng shì zài xiǎoxīnyìyì de lǎngdú zhèxiē kàn bú jiàn de wénzì. Yǔ tíng yǐhòu zhèxiē shūfǎ jiù huì xiāoshī, dàn shítou jìde měi yí cì jiàngyǔ, zhèng rú tā jìde měi yí gè zǒu guò de rén.
text_en: Walking through the old street in the rain, I noticed the water stains on the flagstone road had formed a kind of natural calligraphy. The irregular traces of water spread along the cracks between stones like brushstrokes of some ancient script — some bold and expansive, others delicate and meandering. Each flagstone, worn to different degrees, absorbs rainwater at different rates: the polished smooth ones pool water into mirrors reflecting the gray sky and hurried silhouettes of passersby; the heavily worn stones quickly absorb the moisture, presenting a deep, somber darkness. This alternation of light and dark composes an abstract scroll painting, visible only under specific conditions of light and humidity. Crouching down to examine closely, I found the stones also bore extremely fine grain patterns — the traces of countless feet treading over hundreds of years, time's signature carved into stone. An old man walked past with an umbrella, his cloth shoes making a gentle sound on the wet stones, as if carefully reading aloud these invisible characters. After the rain stops this calligraphy will vanish, but the stone remembers every rainfall, just as it remembers every person who walked across it.

questions.q_zh: 磨得光滑的石板在雨天呈现什么效果？
questions.q_en: What effect do the polished smooth flagstones show in rain?

options.pinyin: shuǐzhū huìjù chéng jìngzi bān de biǎomiàn

questions.explanation: Polished flagstones cannot absorb water, so rain pools on their smooth surfaces into mirror-like sheets that reflect the sky.

questions.q_zh: 石板上的细纹路是怎么来的？
questions.q_en: How did the fine grain patterns on the stones form?

options.text: 几百年来无数双脚踩踏留下的

options.text_en: Left by countless feet over hundreds of years

questions.q_zh: 如果去掉这段话的最后一句，意义会怎样变化？
questions.q_en: If the last sentence were removed, how would the meaning change?

options.text_en: the overall feeling would be incomplete

questions.explanation: The final line ('After the rain stops this calligraphy will vanish, but the...') delivers the thematic payoff; without it, the preceding observations lack their intended resolution.

title: The Hospital Garden Nobody Planned
title_zh: 没有人规划的医院花园
text_zh: 我住院的那三周里，最常去的地方不是食堂，而是住院部后面那个没有人规划的花园。严格来说它不是花园——只是两栋楼之间一块被遗忘的空地，不知道什么时候有人在那里放了几条长凳，种了几棵不知名的灌木。但正因为它不在任何建筑图纸上，没有人来管理，它反而发展出了一种自发的生态系统。住院的病人们根据自己的习惯占据了不同的时段和区域：早晨属于做康复训练的骨科病人，他们在小路上缓慢地来回走动，有的拄着拐杖，有的扶着栏杆；上午属于化疗后出来透气的肿瘤科病人，他们通常戴着帽子或头巾，坐在最晒得到太阳的那条长凳上；下午属于家属——那些在病房里待了太久、需要出来呼吸一下的人。这些群体之间有一种微妙的默契：没有人大声说话，没有人占据别人习惯的位置，偶尔交换一个点头或者一个「今天感觉怎么样？」的问候。这个花园教会了我一件事：最好的公共空间不是被设计出来的，而是被需要它的人共同塑造出来的。管理的缺失反而创造了一种更深层次的秩序——基于同理心和共同处境的秩序。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhùyuàn de nà sān zhōu lǐ, zuì cháng qù de dìfāng bú shì shítáng, ér shì zhùyuàn bù hòumiàn nàge méiyǒu rén guīhuà de huāyuán. Yángé lái shuō tā bú shì huāyuán — zhǐ shì liǎng dòng lóu zhī jiān yí kuài bèi yíwàng de kòngdì, bù zhīdào shénme shíhou yǒu rén zài nàlǐ fàng le jǐ tiáo cháng dèng, zhǒng le jǐ kē bù zhīmíng de guànmù. Dàn zhèng yīnwèi tā bú zài rènhé jiànzhù túzhǐ shàng, méiyǒu rén lái guǎnlǐ, tā fǎn'ér fāzhǎn chū le yì zhǒng zìfā de shēngtài xìtǒng. Zhùyuàn de bìngrén men gēnjù zìjǐ de xíguàn zhànjù le bùtóng de shíduàn hé qūyù: zǎochén shǔyú zuò kāngfù xùnliàn de gǔkē bìngrén, tāmen zài xiǎo lù shàng huǎnmàn de láihuí zǒudòng, yǒu de zhǔ zhe guǎizhàng, yǒu de fú zhe lángǎn; shàngwǔ shǔyú huàliáo hòu chūlái tòu qì de zhǒngliú kē bìngrén, tāmen tōngcháng dài zhe màozi huò tóujīn, zuò zài zuì shài de dào tàiyáng de nà tiáo cháng dèng shàng; xiàwǔ shǔyú jiāshǔ — nàxiē zài bìngfáng lǐ dāi le tài jiǔ, xūyào chūlái hūxī yíxià de rén. Zhèxiē qúntǐ zhī jiān yǒu yì zhǒng wēimiào de mòqì: méiyǒu rén dàshēng shuōhuà, méiyǒu rén zhànjù biérén xíguàn de wèizhi, ǒu'ěr jiāohuàn yí gè diǎntóu huòzhě yí gè「jīntiān gǎnjué zěnmeyàng?」de wènhòu. Zhège huāyuán jiāohuì le wǒ yí jiàn shì: zuì hǎo de gōnggòng kōngjiān bú shì bèi shèjì chūlái de, ér shì bèi xūyào tā de rén gòngtóng sùzào chūlái de. Guǎnlǐ de quēshī fǎn'ér chuàngzào le yì zhǒng gèng shēn céngcì de zhìxù — jīyú tónglǐ xīn hé gòngtóng chǔjìng de zhìxù.
text_en: During my three weeks in the hospital, the place I visited most wasn't the cafeteria but the unplanned garden behind the inpatient building. Strictly speaking it wasn't a garden — just a forgotten plot between two buildings where someone at some point placed a few benches and planted some nameless shrubs. But precisely because it appeared on no architectural blueprint and nobody managed it, it had developed a spontaneous ecosystem. Hospitalized patients occupied different time slots and zones according to their habits: mornings belonged to orthopedic patients doing rehabilitation exercises, slowly walking back and forth on the paths, some with crutches, some holding railings; mid-mornings belonged to oncology patients stepping out for air after chemotherapy, usually wearing hats or headscarves, sitting on the bench that caught the most sun; afternoons belonged to family members — people who'd spent too long in patient rooms and needed to breathe. Between these groups existed a subtle unspoken understanding: nobody spoke loudly, nobody took someone else's habitual spot, occasionally exchanging a nod or a 'how are you feeling today?' This garden taught me something: the best public spaces aren't designed — they're collectively shaped by the people who need them. The absence of management actually created a deeper order — one based on empathy and shared circumstance.

questions.q_zh: 上午这个空间属于谁？
questions.q_en: Who does the space belong to in the mid-morning?

options.pinyin: huàliáo hòu de zhǒngliú kē bìngrén

options.pinyin: bèi xūyào tā de rén gòngtóng sùzào chūlái de
options.text_en: Collectively shaped by people who need them

title: The Watchmaker's Last Repair
title_zh: 钟表匠的最后一次修理
text_zh: 胡同尽头有一家钟表修理铺，招牌已经褪色得几乎看不清字了。铺子的主人姓沈，今年七十八岁，是这条街上最后一个钟表匠。我去他那里修一块父亲留给我的老怀表的时候，他接过表仔细端详了很久，然后用一种近乎虔诚的语气说「这是七十年代上海牌的机芯，现在已经不生产了」。他把怀表放在工作台上，戴上一副放大镜，用一把比牙签还细的螺丝刀开始拆卸后盖。他的手指布满了皱纹和老茧，但动作的精准度令人惊叹——每一个零件被取出的时候他都轻轻放在一块黑色的绒布上，按照拆卸的顺序排列整齐。他指着一个极小的齿轮说「你看，这个游丝断了一根，所以走时不准。这个零件我这里还有最后一个，换完就真的没有了」。整个修理过程花了两个小时，期间他几乎没有说话，只有金属零件轻碰的声响和他均匀的呼吸声。修好以后他把表盖合上，放在耳边听了几秒钟，然后满意地点了点头。他收了我八十块钱，我觉得太少了。他说「修表不是为了赚钱，是为了让时间继续走下去。这块表还能走，比我扔掉一个零件要重要得多」。走出铺子的时候我回头看了一眼——他已经又低下头在修另一块表了，窗外的阳光照在他花白的头发上，像是在为一个即将消失的行当镀上最后一层金。
text_pinyin: Hútòng jìntóu yǒu yì jiā zhōngbiǎo xiūlǐ pù, zhāopái yǐjīng tuìsè de jīhū kàn bù qīng zì le. Pùzi de zhǔrén xìng Shěn, jīnnián qīshíbā suì, shì zhè tiáo jiē shàng zuìhòu yí gè zhōngbiǎo jiàng. Wǒ qù tā nàlǐ xiū yí kuài fùqīn liú gěi wǒ de lǎo huáibiǎo de shíhou, tā jiē guò biǎo zǐxì duānxiáng le hěn jiǔ, ránhòu yòng yì zhǒng jìnhū qiánchéng de yǔqì shuō「zhè shì qīshí niándài Shànghǎi pái de jīxīn, xiànzài yǐjīng bù shēngchǎn le」. Tā bǎ huáibiǎo fàng zài gōngzuòtái shàng, dài shàng yí fù fàngdà jìng, yòng yì bǎ bǐ yáqiān hái xì de luósīdāo kāishǐ chāixiè hòu gài. Tā de shǒuzhǐ bùmǎn le zhòuwén hé lǎojiǎn, dàn dòngzuò de jīngzhǔn dù lìng rén jīngtàn — měi yí gè língjiàn bèi qǔ chū de shíhou tā dōu qīngqīng fàng zài yí kuài hēisè de róngbù shàng, ànzhào chāixiè de shùnxù páiliè zhěngqí. Tā zhǐ zhe yí gè jí xiǎo de chǐlún shuō「nǐ kàn, zhège yóusī duàn le yì gēn, suǒyǐ zǒushí bù zhǔn. Zhège língjiàn wǒ zhèlǐ hái yǒu zuìhòu yí gè, huàn wán jiù zhēn de méiyǒu le」. Zhěnggè xiūlǐ guòchéng huā le liǎng gè xiǎoshí, qījiān tā jīhū méiyǒu shuōhuà, zhǐ yǒu jīnshǔ língjiàn qīng pèng de shēngxiǎng hé tā jūnyún de hūxī shēng. Xiū hǎo yǐhòu tā bǎ biǎo gài hé shàng, fàng zài ěr biān tīng le jǐ miǎozhōng, ránhòu mǎnyì de diǎn le diǎn tóu. Tā shōu le wǒ bāshí kuài qián, wǒ juéde tài shǎo le. Tā shuō「xiū biǎo bú shì wèi le zhuàn qián, shì wèi le ràng shíjiān jìxù zǒu xiàqù. Zhè kuài biǎo hái néng zǒu, bǐ wǒ rēng diào yí gè língjiàn yào zhòngyào de duō」. Zǒu chū pùzi de shíhou wǒ huítóu kàn le yì yǎn — tā yǐjīng yòu dīxià tóu zài xiū lìng yí kuài biǎo le, chuāng wài de yángguāng zhào zài tā huābái de tóufa shàng, xiàng shì zài wèi yí gè jíjiāng xiāoshī de hángdang dù shàng zuìhòu yì céng jīn.
text_en: At the end of the hutong there's a watch repair shop whose sign has faded to near-illegibility. The owner, surnamed Shen, is seventy-eight — the last watchmaker on this street. When I brought my father's old pocket watch, he studied it and said reverently, 'this is a Shanghai brand movement from the seventies — they stopped making these.' He put on a magnifying loupe and began removing the back cover with a screwdriver thinner than a toothpick. His fingers were wrinkled and callused, but precise — each component placed gently on black velvet, arranged in disassembly order. He pointed to a tiny gear: 'this hairspring has broken. I have the last replacement; once it's used, there truly won't be any more.' The repair took two hours. He barely spoke — only the clinks of metal parts and his steady breathing. After finishing, he held the watch to his ear, then nodded. He charged eighty yuan. I said it was too little. He said, 'repairing watches isn't about money — it's about keeping time moving forward. This watch can still run, and that matters more than me hoarding a spare part.' Walking out I glanced back — he had already lowered his head to the next watch, window sunlight falling on his gray hair like a final gilding on a disappearing trade.

questions.q_zh: 怀表走时不准的原因是什么？
questions.q_en: Why wasn't the pocket watch keeping accurate time?
questions.explanation: The watchmaker points to a tiny gear and diagnoses directly: the hairspring has broken, causing inaccurate timekeeping — a specific mechanical failure, not battery or water damage.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的'留白'（没说的部分）有什么作用？
questions.q_en: What role does the 'white space' (what's unsaid) play in this passage?

options.text_en: gives the reader room to imagine

options.text: 说明作者写作能力不足
options.text_en: shows the author's lack of skill

questions.explanation: The passage leaves the watchmaker's feelings and the craft's future unspoken; the reader infers elegy from concrete details of care and finality.

questions.q_zh: 作者用什么比喻来形容阳光照在沈师傅头发上？
questions.q_en: What metaphor does the author use for sunlight on Master Shen's hair?

options.text: 为即将消失的行当镀上最后一层金
options.pinyin: wèi jíjiāng xiāoshī de hángdang dù shàng zuìhòu yì céng jīn
options.text_en: A final gilding on a trade about to disappear

options.pinyin: xiàng làzhú kuài yào miè le
options.text_en: Like a candle about to go out

questions.explanation: The closing image — sunlight on his gray hair like a final gilding on a disappearing trade — explicitly links the visual moment to the larger metaphor of craft extinction.

title: The Accent I Can't Lose
text_zh: 我在北京生活了十五年，普通话已经说得很标准了——至少我自己这么认为。直到有一次在饭局上，一个语言学专业的朋友突然说「你说'事情'的时候，声母会带一点点舌尖后音的变异，这是典型的川东口音特征」。我当时很惊讶，因为我离开重庆的时候才十八岁，一直以为那些语音痕迹早已被北京的空气稀释干净了。回家以后我录了一段自己说话的音频反复听，终于发现了那些隐藏在标准普通话外壳下的微妙偏差：某些翘舌音不够饱满，某些声调在连读时会出现四川话特有的降调模式，还有一些词汇的语调轮廓暴露了我的来历。这些痕迹如此之细微，以至于日常交流中几乎无人察觉。但对于一个训练有素的耳朵来说，它们就像指纹一样清晰。我曾经想过要彻底矫正这些残余的口音，但后来放弃了。因为我意识到，这些微小的语音偏差恰恰是我身份的地层学证据——它们记录了我从哪里来，经历了什么样的语言环境，最终成为了什么样的说话者。消除口音就等于抹去一部分自己的历史，而我不愿意用完美的标准化来交换那份独特的来历。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài Běijīng shēnghuó le shíwǔ nián, pǔtōnghuà yǐjīng shuō de hěn biāozhǔn le — zhìshǎo wǒ zìjǐ zhème rènwéi. Zhídào yǒu yí cì zài fànjú shàng, yí gè yǔyánxué zhuānyè de péngyou tūrán shuō「nǐ shuō 'shìqing' de shíhou, shēngmǔ huì dài yìdiǎndiǎn shéjiān hòu yīn de biànyì, zhè shì diǎnxíng de Chuān dōng kǒuyīn tèzhēng」. Wǒ dāngshí hěn jīngyà, yīnwèi wǒ líkāi Chóngqìng de shíhou cái shíbā suì, yìzhí yǐwéi nàxiē yǔyīn hénjì zǎo yǐ bèi Běijīng de kōngqì xīshì gānjìng le. Huí jiā yǐhòu wǒ lù le yí duàn zìjǐ shuōhuà de yīnpín fǎnfù tīng, zhōngyú fāxiàn le nàxiē yǐncáng zài biāozhǔn pǔtōnghuà wàiké xià de wēimiào piānchā: mǒuxiē qiào shé yīn bú gòu bǎomǎn, mǒuxiē shēngdiào zài liándú shí huì chūxiàn Sìchuān huà tèyǒu de jiàngdiào móshì, hái yǒu yìxiē cíhuì de yǔdiào lúnkuò bàolù le wǒ de láilì. Zhèxiē hénjì rúcǐ zhī xìwēi, yǐzhìyú rìcháng jiāoliú zhōng jīhū wú rén chájué. Dàn duìyú yí gè xùnliàn yǒu sù de ěrduo lái shuō, tāmen jiù xiàng zhǐwén yíyàng qīngxī. Wǒ céngjīng xiǎng guò yào chèdǐ jiǎozhèng zhèxiē cányú de kǒuyīn, dàn hòulái fàngqì le. Yīnwèi wǒ yìshí dào, zhèxiē wēixiǎo de yǔyīn piānchā qiàqià shì wǒ shēnfèn de dìcéngxué zhèngjù — tāmen jìlù le wǒ cóng nǎlǐ lái, jīnglì le shénme yàng de yǔyán huánjìng, zuìzhōng chéngwéi le shénme yàng de shuōhuà zhě. Xiāochú kǒuyīn jiù děngyú mǒ qù yí bùfen zìjǐ de lìshǐ, ér wǒ bú yuànyì yòng wánměi de biāozhǔnhuà lái jiāohuàn nà fèn dútè de láilì.
text_en: I've lived in Beijing for fifteen years, and my Mandarin has become very standard — at least I thought so. Until at a dinner once, a friend who studied linguistics suddenly said, 'when you say "shìqing," your initial consonant carries a slight retroflexive variation — that's a classic eastern Sichuan accent feature.' I was shocked, because I left Chongqing at eighteen and always assumed those phonetic traces had long been diluted clean by Beijing's air. Back home I recorded myself speaking and listened repeatedly, finally discovering those subtle deviations hidden beneath the shell of standard Mandarin: certain retroflex sounds weren't full enough, certain tones in connected speech showed the falling patterns unique to Sichuan dialect, and the intonation contours of certain words betrayed my origins. These traces were so subtle that almost nobody noticed in daily conversation. But to a trained ear, they were as clear as fingerprints. I once thought about completely correcting these residual accents, but eventually gave up. Because I realized these tiny phonetic deviations were precisely the stratigraphic evidence of my identity — they recorded where I came from, what linguistic environments I passed through, and what kind of speaker I ultimately became. Erasing an accent equals erasing part of one's own history, and I wasn't willing to trade that unique origin for perfect standardization.

questions.q_zh: 谁发现了作者口音的特征？
questions.q_en: Who discovered the author's accent features?
questions.explanation: A linguist friend at a dinner party was the first to identify and name the specific tonal features of the author's regional accent.

questions.q_zh: 作者通过叙述想引发读者什么思考？
questions.q_en: What reflection does the author want to provoke in the reader?

options.text: 对日常生活更深的理解
options.pinyin: duì rìcháng shēnghuó gèng shēn de lǐjiě

questions.explanation: By building from concrete observation to philosophical generalization, the author models attentive seeing — inviting the reader to look more carefully at their own daily patterns.

questions.q_zh: 作者最终为什么不矫正口音了？
questions.q_en: Why did the author ultimately not correct the accent?

options.text: 不愿意用标准化交换独特的来历
options.pinyin: bú yuànyì yòng biāozhǔnhuà jiāohuàn dútè de láilì
options.text_en: Unwilling to trade unique origins for standardization

questions.explanation: The passage explains: 'Erasing an accent equals erasing part of one's own history, and I...' — this reasoning directly supports the answer; alternatives are unsupported.

title: The Department Meeting Minutes Nobody Reads
title_zh: 没有人读的部门会议纪要
text_zh: 我在公司负责记录部门会议纪要已经两年了。每次会议结束后我都会认真整理讨论内容、决议事项和责任分工，然后发送到全组邮件里。邮件系统的已读回执显示，四十个收件人中平均只有七个人会打开它，而真正从头到尾阅读的人据我估计不超过两个。尽管如此，如果我哪一次忘了发送，至少有十个人会来问我「这次的会议纪要怎么还没发？」这形成了一个精妙的悖论：人们不读它，但需要知道它存在。它的价值在于「万一有争议的时候可以翻出来查」，虽然在我两年的任期内，真正因为争议而翻查纪要的情况只发生过一次。那一次的结论是：纪要里写的和大家记忆中的都不一样，因为我当时也走神了，记了一个错误的数字。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài gōngsī fùzé jìlù bùmén huìyì jìyào yǐjīng liǎng nián le. Zhè shì yí gè yǒuqù de juésè, yīnwèi tā ràng wǒ fāxiàn le yí gè zǔzhī xíngwéixué de mìmì: jīhū méiyǒu rén yuèdú huìyì jìyào. Měi cì huìyì jiéshù hòu wǒ dōu huì rènzhēn zhěnglǐ tǎolùn nèiróng, juéyì shìxiàng hé zérèn fēngōng, ránhòu fāsòng dào quán zǔ yóujiàn lǐ. Yóujiàn xìtǒng de yǐ dú huízhí xiǎnshì, sìshí gè shōujiàn rén zhōng píngjūn zhǐ yǒu qī gè rén huì dǎkāi tā, ér zhēnzhèng cóng tóu dào wěi yuèdú de rén jù wǒ gūjì bù chāoguò liǎng gè. Jǐnguǎn rúcǐ, rúguǒ wǒ nǎ yí cì wàng le fāsòng, zhìshǎo yǒu shí gè rén huì lái wèn wǒ「zhè cì de huìyì jìyào zěnme hái méi fā?」Zhè xíngchéng le yí gè jīngmiào de bèilùn: rénmen bú dú tā, dàn xūyào zhīdào tā cúnzài. Huìyì jìyào de zhēnzhèng gōngnéng bú shì chuándì xìnxī — yīnwèi zhòngyào de xìnxī zǎo yǐ zài huìyì shàng kǒutóu chuándá le — ér shì chōngdāng yì zhǒng zǔzhī de ānquán wǎng. Tā de jiàzhí zàiyú「wànyī yǒu zhēngyì de shíhou kěyǐ fān chūlái chá」, suīrán zài wǒ liǎng nián de rènqī nèi, zhēnzhèng yīnwèi zhēngyì ér fān chá jìyào de qíngkuàng zhǐ fāshēng guò yí cì. Nà yí cì de jiélùn shì: jìyào lǐ xiě de hé dàjiā jìyì zhōng de dōu bù yíyàng, yīnwèi wǒ dāngshí yě zǒu shén le, jì le yí gè cuòwù de shùzì. Cóngcǐ yǐhòu wǒ jì huìyì jìyào bǐ yǐqián gèng rènzhēn le — dào bú shì yīnwèi yǒu rén zài dú, ér shì yīnwèi nà wéiyī bèi fān chá de yí cì ràng wǒ yìshí dào, jíshǐ shì méiyǒu rén dú de wénjiàn yě yīnggāi shì zhǔnquè de.
text_en: I've been responsible for recording department meeting minutes at my company for two years now. After every meeting I carefully organize discussion content, resolutions, and task assignments, then send them to the whole group email. The read receipts show that of forty recipients, an average of only seven open it, and those who truly read it start to finish number, by my estimate, no more than two. Yet if I ever forget to send them, at least ten people come asking 'why haven't the minutes been sent yet?' This creates an elegant paradox: people don't read them but need to know they exist. Their value lies in 'being available to check if disputes arise,' though in my two-year tenure, minutes were actually consulted due to a dispute exactly once. That time the conclusion was: what the minutes said didn't match anyone's memory either, because I had also zoned out and recorded a wrong number.

questions.q_zh: 从职业选择可以看出什么？
questions.q_en: What can we infer from the career choice?
questions.explanation: Persistent diligence despite near-zero readership reveals that the behavior stems from personal values — character, not audience, drives the conscientiousness.

questions.q_zh: 这段话对工作态度的看法是什么？
questions.q_en: What view of work attitude does this passage present?

options.text_en: work is less important than life

questions.explanation: The author's response to their single error — becoming more careful, not less engaged — shows conscientiousness matters as a value in itself, independent of recognition.

questions.q_zh: 唯一一次翻查纪要的结果是什么？
questions.q_en: What was the result of the one time minutes were consulted?

options.pinyin: zhèngmíng le jìyào hěn yǒu yòng

options.text: 纪要和大家记忆都不一样，因为作者也记错了
options.pinyin: jìyào hé dàjiā jìyì dōu bù yíyàng, yīnwèi zuòzhě yě jì cuò le
options.text_en: Minutes didn't match anyone's memory because the author also made an error

options.pinyin: dàjiā juédìng bú zài jì jìyào
options.text_en: Everyone decided to stop keeping minutes

questions.explanation: When the minutes were finally consulted, they contradicted everyone's memory — and it turned out the author had recorded them incorrectly too.

title: The Philosophy of Soup Stock
text_zh: 一锅好的高汤需要至少八个小时的慢炖。我外婆是做高汤的高手，她的厨房里永远有一锅汤在炉子上小火慢熬。她说高汤的秘诀不在于放了什么，而在于愿意等多久。她用的材料并不稀奇：老母鸡、猪骨、姜片、葱段——这些在任何菜市场都能买到。但她处理这些材料的方式有一种近乎仪式感的讲究：鸡要选两年以上的老母鸡，因为只有它的骨头才够硬，味道才够浓；猪骨要先焯水去血沫，然后用冷水重新开始煮；姜要拍碎而不是切片，这样味道释放得更充分。最关键的一步是火候：大火煮沸以后转最小火，保持水面只有轻微的波动——她称之为「虾眼泡」，意思是气泡只有虾的眼睛那么大。在这个状态下，汤会慢慢变得浑厚、金黄、透亮，带着一种无法用言语描述的鲜味层次。大火出来的是白汤，混浊而猛烈；小火出来的是清汤，温润而深邃。这个区别就像说话：大嗓门的人让你听见的是声量，轻声细语的人让你听见的是内容。外婆已经去世五年了，我试过无数次复制她的高汤，味道永远差那么一点。也许缺少的不是配方而是那种愿意花八个小时等待一锅汤的耐心——以及只有几十年的经验才能培养出来的、对「虾眼泡」的精确判断。
text_pinyin: Yì guō hǎo de gāotāng xūyào zhìshǎo bā gè xiǎoshí de màn dùn. Wǒ wàipó shì zuò gāotāng de gāoshǒu, tā de chúfáng lǐ yǒngyuǎn yǒu yì guō tāng zài lúzi shàng xiǎo huǒ màn áo. Tā shuō gāotāng de mìjué bú zàiyú fàng le shénme, ér zàiyú yuànyì děng duō jiǔ. Tā yòng de cáiliào bìng bù xīqí: lǎo mǔjī, zhūgǔ, jiāng piàn, cōng duàn — zhèxiē zài rènhé cài shìchǎng dōu néng mǎi dào. Dàn tā chǔlǐ zhèxiē cáiliào de fāngshì yǒu yì zhǒng jìnhū yíshì gǎn de jiǎngjiu: jī yào xuǎn liǎng nián yǐshàng de lǎo mǔjī, yīnwèi zhǐ yǒu tā de gǔtou cái gòu yìng, wèidào cái gòu nóng; zhūgǔ yào xiān chāo shuǐ qù xuè mò, ránhòu yòng lěng shuǐ chóngxīn kāishǐ zhǔ; jiāng yào pāi suì ér bú shì qiē piàn, zhèyàng wèidào shìfàng de gèng chōngfèn. Zuì guānjiàn de yí bù shì huǒhòu: dà huǒ zhǔ fèi yǐhòu zhuǎn zuì xiǎo huǒ, bǎochí shuǐmiàn zhǐ yǒu qīngwēi de bōdòng — tā chēng zhī wéi「xiā yǎn pào」, yìsi shì qìpào zhǐ yǒu xiā de yǎnjing nàme dà. Zài zhège zhuàngtài xià, tāng huì mànmàn biàn de húnhòu, jīnhuáng, tòu liàng, dài zhe yì zhǒng wúfǎ yòng yányǔ miáoshù de xiānwèi céngcì. Dà huǒ chūlái de shì bái tāng, hùnzhuó ér měngliè; xiǎo huǒ chūlái de shì qīngtāng, wēnrùn ér shēnsuì. Zhège qūbié jiù xiàng shuōhuà: dà sǎngmén de rén ràng nǐ tīng jiàn de shì shēngliàng, qīng shēng xì yǔ de rén ràng nǐ tīng jiàn de shì nèiróng. Wàipó yǐjīng qùshì wǔ nián le, wǒ shì guò wúshù cì fùzhì tā de gāotāng, wèidào yǒngyuǎn chà nàme yìdiǎn. Yěxǔ quēshǎo de bú shì pèifāng ér shì nà zhǒng yuànyì huā bā gè xiǎoshí děngdài yì guō tāng de nàixīn — yǐjí zhǐ yǒu jǐ shí nián de jīngyàn cái néng péiyǎng chūlái de, duì「xiā yǎn pào」de jīngquè pànduàn.
text_en: A good stock requires at least eight hours of slow simmering. My grandmother was a master — her kitchen always had a pot gently simmering. She said the secret isn't what you put in, but how long you're willing to wait. Her ingredients were ordinary: old hen, pork bones, ginger, scallion. But she handled them with ritualistic precision: the hen had to be over two years old, bones hard enough for full flavor. Pork bones were blanched first, then started again in cold water. Ginger was smashed, not sliced. The crucial step was heat control: after boiling, reduce to the lowest flame, keeping the surface barely moving — she called these 'shrimp-eye bubbles,' bubbles only as big as a shrimp's eye. The stock slowly becomes rich, golden, translucent. High heat produces white stock, cloudy and vigorous; low heat produces clear stock, gentle and profound. Like speaking: a loud person makes you hear volume; a soft-spoken person makes you hear content. Grandmother passed away five years ago. I've tried countless times to replicate her stock, but the taste always falls short. What's missing isn't the recipe but the willingness to wait eight hours — and the judgment of 'shrimp-eye bubbles' that only decades can cultivate.

title: The Elevator Mirror
text_zh: 写字楼的电梯里有一面全身镜。大多数人认为它的功能是让人在上班前最后检查一下仪容仪表——整理领带、抚平头发、确认衬衫有没有塞好。但我观察了数百次电梯里的人与镜子之间的关系后，发现事情远比这复杂。首先，几乎所有人进入电梯后都会不自觉地瞟一眼镜子，但大部分人会迅速移开目光，仿佛不愿意被自己或他人发现正在看自己。其次，独自乘坐电梯时和有其他人在场时，人们与镜子的互动方式截然不同：独处时，人们会认真审视自己，调整表情，甚至练习即将进行的演讲的开场白；有同伴时，看镜子的行为几乎完全消失，取而代之的是盯着楼层数字或者手机屏幕。还有一个微妙的现象：电梯里如果只有两个陌生人，镜子反而增加了尴尬——因为即使你没有直视对方，你们也能通过镜子的反射看到彼此，而双方都意识到了这一点。这面镜子揭示了一个关于自我意识的悖论：我们渴望看清自己，但只敢在没有观众的时候这样做。在他人面前，我们宁愿相信自己看起来还不错，也不愿意冒险验证这个假设。
text_pinyin: Xiězìlóu de diàntī lǐ yǒu yí miàn quán shēn jìng. Dà duōshù rén rènwéi tā de gōngnéng shì ràng rén zài shàngbān qián zuìhòu jiǎnchá yíxià yíróng yíbiǎo — zhěnglǐ lǐngdài, fǔpíng tóufa, quèrèn chènshān yǒu méiyǒu sāi hǎo. Dàn wǒ guānchá le shù bǎi cì diàntī lǐ de rén yǔ jìngzi zhī jiān de guānxi hòu, fāxiàn shìqing yuǎn bǐ zhè fùzá. Shǒuxiān, jīhū suǒyǒu rén jìnrù diàntī hòu dōu huì bù zìjué de piǎo yì yǎn jìngzi, dàn dà bùfen rén huì xùnsù yí kāi mùguāng, fǎngfú bú yuànyì bèi zìjǐ huò tārén fāxiàn zhèngzài kàn zìjǐ. Qícì, dúzì chéngzuò diàntī shí hé yǒu qítā rén zàichǎng shí, rénmen yǔ jìngzi de hùdòng fāngshì jiéran bùtóng: dúchù shí, rénmen huì rènzhēn shěnshì zìjǐ, tiáozhěng biǎoqíng, shènzhì liànxí jíjiāng jìnxíng de yǎnjiǎng de kāichǎngbái; yǒu tóngbàn shí, kàn jìngzi de xíngwéi jīhū wánquán xiāoshī, qǔ ér dài zhī de shì dīng zhe lóucéng shùzì huòzhě shǒujī píngmù. Hái yǒu yí gè wēimiào de xiànxiàng: diàntī lǐ rúguǒ zhǐ yǒu liǎng gè mòshēngrén, jìngzi fǎn'ér zēngjiā le gāngà — yīnwèi jíshǐ nǐ méiyǒu zhí shì duìfāng, nǐmen yě néng tōngguò jìngzi de fǎnshè kàn dào bǐcǐ, ér shuāngfāng dōu yìshí dào le zhè yì diǎn. Zhè miàn jìngzi jiēshì le yí gè guānyú zìwǒ yìshí de bèilùn: wǒmen kěwàng kàn qīng zìjǐ, dàn zhǐ gǎn zài méiyǒu guānzhòng de shíhou zhèyàng zuò. Zài tārén miànqián, wǒmen nìngyuàn xiāngxìn zìjǐ kàn qǐlái hái búcuò, yě bú yuànyì màoxiǎn yànzhèng zhège jiǎshè.
text_en: The office building elevator has a full-length mirror. Most people think its function is to let people do one last appearance check before work — straighten a tie, smooth hair, confirm a shirt is properly tucked. But after observing hundreds of interactions between people and the mirror in the elevator, I found things far more complex. First, almost everyone entering the elevator unconsciously glances at the mirror, but most quickly look away, as if unwilling to be caught — by themselves or others — looking at themselves. Second, the way people interact with the mirror differs drastically when alone versus when others are present: alone, people seriously examine themselves, adjust expressions, even practice opening lines for upcoming presentations; with companions, mirror-looking nearly vanishes, replaced by staring at floor numbers or phone screens. There's another subtle phenomenon: when only two strangers are in the elevator, the mirror actually increases awkwardness — because even without looking directly at each other, both can see the other through the reflection, and both realize this. This mirror reveals a paradox of self-awareness: we desire to see ourselves clearly but only dare to do so without an audience. In front of others, we'd rather believe we look fine than risk verifying that assumption.

title: Why Bookstores Smell Like Home
title_zh: 为什么书店闻起来像家
text_zh: 有些人走进书店会立刻感到一种难以言说的放松和安全感。这种反应不仅仅是心理上的——它实际上有一个嗅觉层面的科学解释。纸张在缓慢氧化的过程中会释放出一种叫做香草醛的化学物质，它的气味类似于香草冰淇淋。与此同时，书页中的纤维素降解后会产生微量的呋喃类化合物，闻起来带有一种温暖的、焦糖般的甜味。这两种气味的组合恰好与许多人童年记忆中家的味道高度重合：厨房里烤蛋糕的香气、壁炉旁木头的气息、祖母书房里陈年书籍的幽香。嗅觉是所有感官中与情感记忆联系最紧密的一种——气味信号直接通过嗅球传入边缘系统的海马体和杏仁核，绕过了大脑皮层的理性分析，因此气味引发的记忆往往比视觉或听觉记忆更加鲜明、更加情感化。这就是为什么走进一家旧书店的那一瞬间你可能突然想起了祖父母的客厅，而这种联想是如此之快以至于你的意识来不及追溯它的源头——你只是感到「安心」，却不知道为什么。电子书的普及正在让这种体验逐渐消失。一本电子书没有气味，因此它也无法成为通往情感记忆的钥匙。我们在获得了便捷的同时失去了一个感官维度，而这个维度恰恰是阅读体验中最不可替代的部分之一。
text_pinyin: Yǒuxiē rén zǒu jìn shūdiàn huì lìkè gǎndào yì zhǒng nányǐ yán shuō de fàngsōng hé ānquángǎn. Zhè zhǒng fǎnyìng bù jǐnjǐn shì xīnlǐ shàng de — tā shíjì shàng yǒu yí gè xiùjué céngmiàn de kēxué jiěshì. Zhǐzhāng zài huǎnmàn yǎnghuà de guòchéng zhōng huì shìfàng chū yì zhǒng jiào zuò xiāngcǎo quán de huàxué wùzhì, tā de qìwèi lèisì yú xiāngcǎo bīngqílín. Yǔ cǐ tóngshí, shūyè zhōng de xiānwéi sù jiàngjǐe hòu huì chǎnshēng wēiliàng de fūnán lèi huàhé wù, wén qǐlái dài yǒu yì zhǒng wēnnuǎn de, jiāotáng bān de tiánwèi. Zhè liǎng zhǒng qìwèi de zǔhé qiàhǎo yǔ xǔduō rén tóngnián jìyì zhōng jiā de wèidào gāodù chónghé: chúfáng lǐ kǎo dàngāo de xiāngqì, bìlú páng mùtou de qìxī, zǔmǔ shūfáng lǐ chénnián shūjí de yōuxiāng. Xiùjué shì suǒyǒu gǎnguān zhōng yǔ qínggǎn jìyì liánxì zuì jǐnmì de yì zhǒng — qìwèi xìnhào zhíjiē tōngguò xiùqiú chuán rù biānyuán xìtǒng de hǎimǎ tǐ hé xìngrénhé, rào guò le dànǎo píchéng de lǐxìng fēnxī, yīncǐ qìwèi yǐnfā de jìyì wǎngwǎng bǐ shìjué huò tīngjué jìyì gèngjiā xiānmíng, gèngjiā qínggǎn huà. Zhè jiù shì wèi shénme zǒu jìn yì jiā jiù shūdiàn de nà yí shùnjiān nǐ kěnéng tūrán xiǎngqǐ le zǔfùmǔ de kètīng, ér zhè zhǒng liánxiǎng shì rúcǐ zhī kuài yǐzhìyú nǐ de yìshí lái bù jí zhuīsù tā de yuántóu — nǐ zhǐ shì gǎndào「ānxīn」, què bù zhīdào wèi shénme. Diànzǐ shū de pǔjí zhèngzài ràng zhè zhǒng tǐyàn zhújiàn xiāoshī. Yì běn diànzǐ shū méiyǒu qìwèi, yīncǐ tā yě wúfǎ chéngwéi tōng wǎng qínggǎn jìyì de yàoshi. Wǒmen zài huòdé le biànjié de tóngshí shīqù le yí gè gǎnguān wéidù, ér zhège wéidù qiàqià shì yuèdú tǐyàn zhōng zuì bùkě tìdài de bùfen zhī yī.
text_en: Some people feel immediate relaxation and safety upon entering a bookstore. This reaction has a scientific explanation. Paper during slow oxidation releases vanillin, whose scent resembles vanilla. Cellulose degradation in book pages produces trace furan compounds with a warm, caramel-like sweetness. These scents overlap with many people's childhood memories: cake baking in the kitchen, fireside wood, aged books in grandmother's study. Smell is the sense most closely linked to emotional memory — olfactory signals pass directly through the olfactory bulb into the limbic system's hippocampus and amygdala, bypassing rational analysis. Scent-triggered memories are more vivid and emotional than visual or auditory ones. This is why entering an old bookstore might make you think of your grandparents' living room — the association happens so fast your consciousness can't trace its source. You just feel 'safe.' E-books are erasing this experience. An e-book has no scent and cannot serve as a key to emotional memory. In gaining convenience we've lost a sensory dimension — one of the most irreplaceable parts of reading.

title: The Night Shift Security Guard's Notebook
text_zh: 我们写字楼的夜班保安姓周，五十三岁，以前是一个中学的地理老师，因为学校合并下岗了。他从不抱怨这份工作，但你能从一些细节看出他是一个内心丰富的人。比如他值班台上永远放着一个软皮笔记本和一支削得很尖的铅笔。有一次我加班到凌晨两点经过大厅，看到他正在笔记本上写字。我好奇地问他在记什么。他有点不好意思地翻开给我看——里面密密麻麻地记录着他每个夜班观察到的各种细节：「十一月七日，大厅东北角的射灯有一盏开始闪烁，估计是接触不良」「十一月十二日，二十三楼加班的人比平时多，外卖骑手九点到十点之间来了七次」「十一月十五日，凌晨一点有一只猫从旋转门的缝隙溜进来，在沙发上睡了两个小时，然后自己走了」。这些记录没有任何实用价值——他并不需要向任何人汇报这些事。他只是在用自己的方式理解和记录这栋楼的夜间生命。他说「当老师的时候我观察学生，现在我观察这栋楼。每栋楼到了晚上都有自己的性格——这栋楼的性格是安静但不冷清，像一个在深夜还亮着灯等你回来的房间」。我突然觉得，这个保安对这栋楼的理解比任何一个白天在里面工作的人都深刻。因为他看到的是它卸下白天所有伪装以后的真实面目。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiězìlóu de yèbān bǎo'ān xìng Zhōu, wǔshísān suì, yǐqián shì yí gè zhōngxué de dìlǐ lǎoshī, yīnwèi xuéxiào hébìng xiàgǎng le. Tā cóng bù bàoyuàn zhè fèn gōngzuò, dàn nǐ néng cóng yìxiē xìjié kàn chū tā shì yí gè nèixīn fēngfù de rén. Bǐrú tā zhíbān tái shàng yǒngyuǎn fàng zhe yí gè ruǎn pí bǐjìběn hé yì zhī xiāo de hěn jiān de qiānbǐ. Yǒu yí cì wǒ jiābān dào língchén liǎng diǎn jīngguò dàtīng, kàn dào tā zhèngzài bǐjìběn shàng xiě zì. Wǒ hàoqí de wèn tā zài jì shénme. Tā yǒudiǎn bù hǎoyìsi de fān kāi gěi wǒ kàn — lǐmiàn mìmìmámá de jìlù zhe tā měi gè yèbān guānchá dào de gè zhǒng xìjié:「shíyī yuè qī rì, dàtīng dōngběi jiǎo de shèdēng yǒu yì zhǎn kāishǐ shǎnshuò, gūjì shì jiēchù bùliáng」「shíyī yuè shí'èr rì, èrshísān lóu jiābān de rén bǐ píngshí duō, wàimài qíshǒu jiǔ diǎn dào shí diǎn zhī jiān lái le qī cì」「shíyī yuè shíwǔ rì, língchén yì diǎn yǒu yì zhī māo cóng xuánzhuǎn mén de fèngxì liū jìnlái, zài shāfā shàng shuì le liǎng gè xiǎoshí, ránhòu zìjǐ zǒu le」. Zhèxiē jìlù méiyǒu rènhé shíyòng jiàzhí — tā bìng bù xūyào xiàng rènhé rén huìbào zhèxiē shì. Tā zhǐ shì zài yòng zìjǐ de fāngshì lǐjiě hé jìlù zhè dòng lóu de yèjiān shēngmìng. Tā shuō「dāng lǎoshī de shíhou wǒ guānchá xuéshēng, xiànzài wǒ guānchá zhè dòng lóu. Měi dòng lóu dào le wǎnshang dōu yǒu zìjǐ de xìnggé — zhè dòng lóu de xìnggé shì ānjìng dàn bù lěngqīng, xiàng yí gè zài shēnyè hái liàng zhe dēng děng nǐ huílái de fángjiān」. Wǒ tūrán juéde, zhège bǎo'ān duì zhè dòng lóu de lǐjiě bǐ rènhé yí gè báitiān zài lǐmiàn gōngzuò de rén dōu shēnkè. Yīnwèi tā kàn dào de shì tā xiè xià báitiān suǒyǒu wěizhuāng yǐhòu de zhēnshí miànmù.
text_en: The night shift security guard at our office building is surnamed Zhou, fifty-three, formerly a geography teacher laid off when his school merged. He never complains, but details reveal a rich inner life. His duty desk always has a leather notebook and a sharp pencil. Once past 2 AM I saw him writing in it. He opened it for me — densely filled with night-shift observations: 'November 7th, a spotlight in the northeast corner started flickering, probably a loose connection.' 'November 12th, more people working late on the 23rd floor, delivery riders came seven times between 9 and 10 PM.' 'November 15th, at 1 AM a cat slipped through the revolving door gap, slept on the sofa two hours, then left.' These records have no practical value — he reports none of it. He's recording this building's nighttime life in his own way. He said, 'when I was a teacher I observed students, now I observe this building. Every building has its own personality at night — this one is quiet but not desolate, like a room that keeps its light on waiting for you to come home.' This guard understands the building more deeply than anyone who works here during the day. He sees its true face after it's shed its daytime pretenses.

questions.explanation: The passage ends at insight rather than prescription, leaving the reader to find parallel meaning in their own life rather than receiving a closed conclusion.

title: The Train Station Announcement Voice
text_zh: 中国火车站的广播系统有一种独特的声学特质——那种略带回声的、字正腔圆的女声，在无数旅客的记忆中已经与「出发」和「到达」形成了巴甫洛夫式的条件反射。我曾经出于好奇研究过这个声音的设计逻辑。首先是语速：火车站广播的语速被精确控制在每分钟约一百八十个字，这比日常对话快百分之十左右，但比新闻播报慢百分之五——这个频率既能传递足够的紧迫感以确保旅客注意，又不至于因为过快而导致听不清。其次是音调：播报声使用的是中频段的女声，因为这个频段在嘈杂环境中的穿透力最强——高频容易被人群的嗡嗡声掩盖，低频则缺乏辨识度。最精妙的设计在于停顿：每一条播报之间有恰好三秒钟的静默，这个长度足以让你的大脑完成从「注意」到「理解」到「判断是否与自己相关」的认知链条。如果更短会造成信息过载，更长则可能让你以为广播已经结束而分散注意力。我在候车大厅里闭上眼睛，让这些声音环绕着我。每一条广播都指向一个目的地，每一个目的地都承载着无数人的期待或不舍。这个声音本身没有情感，但它标记的时刻——出发、到达、别离、重逢——是人生中最充满情感的节点。
text_pinyin: Zhōngguó huǒchē zhàn de guǎngbō xìtǒng yǒu yì zhǒng dútè de shēngxué tèzhì — nà zhǒng lüè dài huíshēng de, zì zhèng qiāng yuán de nǚshēng, zài wúshù lǚkè de jìyì zhōng yǐjīng yǔ「chūfā」hé「dàodá」xíngchéng le Bāfūluòfū shì de tiáojiàn fǎnshè. Wǒ céngjīng chūyú hàoqí yánjiū guò zhège shēngyīn de shèjì luóji. Shǒuxiān shì yǔsù: huǒchē zhàn guǎngbō de yǔsù bèi jīngquè kòngzhì zài měi fēnzhōng yuē yì bǎi bāshí gè zì, zhè bǐ rìcháng duìhuà kuài bǎi fēn zhī shí zuǒyòu, dàn bǐ xīnwén bōbào màn bǎi fēn zhī wǔ — zhège pínlǜ jì néng chuándì zúgòu de jǐnpògǎn yǐ quèbǎo lǚkè zhùyì, yòu bú zhìyú yīnwèi guò kuài ér dǎozhì tīng bù qīng. Qícì shì yīndiào: bōbào shēng shǐyòng de shì zhōng pín duàn de nǚshēng, yīnwèi zhège pín duàn zài cáozá huánjìng zhōng de chuāntòu lì zuì qiáng — gāo pín róngyì bèi rénqún de wēngwēng shēng yǎngài, dī pín zé quēfá biànshí dù. Zuì jīngmiào de shèjì zàiyú tíngdùn: měi yì tiáo bōbào zhī jiān yǒu qiàhǎo sān miǎozhōng de jìngmò, zhège chángdù zúyǐ ràng nǐ de dànǎo wánchéng cóng「zhùyì」dào「lǐjiě」dào「pànduàn shìfǒu yǔ zìjǐ xiāngguān」de rènzhī liántiáo. Rúguǒ gèng duǎn huì zàochéng xìnxī guòzǎi, gèng cháng zé kěnéng ràng nǐ yǐwéi guǎngbō yǐjīng jiéshù ér fēnsàn zhùyìlì. Wǒ zài hòuchē dàtīng lǐ bì shàng yǎnjing, ràng zhèxiē shēngyīn huánrào zhe wǒ. Měi yì tiáo guǎngbō dōu zhǐ xiàng yí gè mùdìdì, měi yí gè mùdìdì dōu chéngzài zhe wúshù rén de qīdài huò bùshě. Zhège shēngyīn běnshēn méiyǒu qínggǎn, dàn tā biāojì de shíkè — chūfā, dàodá, biélí, chóngféng — shì rénshēng zhōng zuì chōngmǎn qínggǎn de jiédiǎn.
text_en: China's train station broadcasting system has a unique acoustic quality — that slightly echoing, perfectly articulated female voice has become a Pavlovian conditioned response associated with 'departure' and 'arrival' in countless travelers' memories. I once researched the design logic of this voice out of curiosity. First is speed: station broadcast speed is precisely controlled at about 180 characters per minute, roughly 10% faster than daily conversation but 5% slower than news broadcasting — this frequency conveys enough urgency to ensure passenger attention without being too fast to understand. Second is pitch: the broadcast uses a mid-range female voice because this frequency has the strongest penetration in noisy environments — high frequencies are easily masked by crowd noise, while low frequencies lack distinctness. The most ingenious design lies in the pauses: exactly three seconds of silence between each announcement, long enough for your brain to complete the cognitive chain from 'attention' to 'comprehension' to 'judging whether it's relevant to me.' Shorter would cause information overload; longer might make you think the broadcast has ended and lose focus. I closed my eyes in the waiting hall and let these sounds surround me. Every announcement points to a destination, every destination carries countless people's anticipation or reluctance. The voice itself has no emotion, but the moments it marks, departure, arrival, parting, reunion — are the most emotionally charged nodes of human life.

title: The Repairman Who Talks to Machines
text_zh: 小区的物业有一个维修师傅姓陈，修水管、修电路、修空调——什么都修。他有一个习惯让所有住户觉得既奇怪又可爱：他会跟机器说话。他修空调的时候会先蹲下来听一会儿压缩机的声音，然后自言自语说「嗯，你是不是缺氟了？别急，我看看」。拧螺丝的时候他会鼓励那颗生锈的螺丝：「别犟了兄弟，松一下嘛」。修完以后他会拍拍机器说「好了好了，以后不要再闹了」。我问他为什么要这么做。他说这不是在跟机器说话，是在跟自己说话。「修东西的时候脑子要集中，嘴巴说出来就不容易走神。而且你说了以后手上就知道下一步该做什么了——就跟医生问病人哪里疼是一个道理」。他还说了一句让我印象很深的话：「其实每台机器坏了都不是突然坏的，都有征兆。你用耳朵听它的声音，用手摸它的温度，用鼻子闻它有没有烧焦的味道——它一直在告诉你哪里不舒服，只是大多数人不愿意听而已」。他走了以后我看着那台刚修好的空调，第一次有一种奇怪的感觉——好像它确实在安静地、满意地运转着，仿佛被一个真正理解它的人照顾过了。
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū de wùyè yǒu yí gè wéixiū shīfu xìng Chén, xiū shuǐguǎn, xiū diànlù, xiū kōngtiáo — shénme dōu xiū. Tā yǒu yí gè xíguàn ràng suǒyǒu zhùhù juéde jì qíguài yòu kě'ài: tā huì gēn jīqì shuōhuà. Tā xiū kōngtiáo de shíhou huì xiān dūn xiàlái tīng yíhuìr yāsuōjī de shēngyīn, ránhòu zìyán zìyǔ shuō「ēn, nǐ shìbúshì quē fú le? Bié jí, wǒ kànkan」. Nǐng luósī de shíhou tā huì gǔlì nà kē shēngxiù de luósī:「bié jiàng le xiōngdì, sōng yíxià ma」. Xiū wán yǐhòu tā huì pāi pāi jīqì shuō「hǎo le hǎo le, yǐhòu bú yào zài nào le」. Wǒ wèn tā wèi shénme yào zhème zuò. Tā shuō zhè bú shì zài gēn jīqì shuōhuà, shì zài gēn zìjǐ shuōhuà.「Xiū dōngxi de shíhou nǎozi yào jízhōng, zuǐba shuō chūlái jiù bù róngyì zǒushén. Érqiě nǐ shuō le yǐhòu shǒu shàng jiù zhīdào xià yí bù gāi zuò shénme le — jiù gēn yīshēng wèn bìngrén nǎlǐ téng shì yí gè dàolǐ」. Tā hái shuō le yí jù ràng wǒ yìnxiàng hěn shēn de huà:「qíshí měi tái jīqì huài le dōu bú shì tūrán huài de, dōu yǒu zhēngzhào. Nǐ yòng ěrduo tīng tā de shēngyīn, yòng shǒu mō tā de wēndù, yòng bízi wén tā yǒu méiyǒu shāojiāo de wèidào — tā yìzhí zài gàosu nǐ nǎlǐ bù shūfu, zhǐ shì dà duōshù rén bú yuànyì tīng éryǐ」. Tā zǒu le yǐhòu wǒ kàn zhe nà tái gāng xiū hǎo de kōngtiáo, dì yī cì yǒu yì zhǒng qíguài de gǎnjué — hǎoxiàng tā quèshí zài ānjìng de, mǎnyì de yùnzhuǎn zhe, fǎngfú bèi yí gè zhēnzhèng lǐjiě tā de rén zhàogù guò le.
text_en: Our compound's repairman, surnamed Chen, fixes pipes, circuits, air conditioners — everything. He has a habit residents find both strange and endearing: he talks to machines. Fixing an air conditioner, he crouches to listen, then murmurs, 'are you low on refrigerant? Let me check.' He encourages a rusty screw: 'stop being stubborn, brother, loosen up.' After finishing he pats the machine: 'no more acting up now.' I asked why. He said he's talking to himself. 'Fixing things requires concentration. Speaking aloud keeps focus. Once you say it, your hands know what to do — same logic as a doctor asking where it hurts.' He also said something striking: 'no machine breaks down suddenly. There are always signs. You listen to its sounds, feel its temperature, smell for burning — it's been telling you where it's uncomfortable, but most people don't bother to listen.' After he left I looked at the repaired air conditioner and felt, for the first time, as if it really was running contentedly — cared for by someone who understood it.

title: The Language of Dust
text_zh: 搬进新公寓以后，我渐渐学会了阅读灰尘。每个角落的灰尘都在讲述一段关于空气流动的故事。窗台上的灰最厚，因为那里是外界微粒进入室内的第一站，像海关一样接纳了来自城市的无数旅客。书架顶部的灰尘则呈现一种均匀的灰白色薄膜，说明那里的空气几乎是静止的，没有人类活动的扰动。最有趣的是厨房抽油烟机上方的灰尘——它带着微微的黄色，混合了油脂和水蒸气，形成了一种黏性的沉积物，记录着每一顿饭的烹饪方式。我用手指在不同表面轻轻划过，灰尘的质地各不相同：有的干燥疏松如细沙，有的潮湿绵密如泥土。每隔两周我会彻底打扫一次，然后观察灰尘重新聚集的过程。它总是先出现在角落和缝隙里，仿佛那些被遗忘的空间对微粒有一种特殊的引力。灰尘是沉默的编年史家，它忠实地记录着一个空间里发生的一切——只要你愿意俯下身去阅读。
text_pinyin: Bān jìn xīn gōngyù yǐhòu, wǒ jiànjiàn xuéhuì le yuèdú huīchén. Měi gè jiǎoluò de huīchén dōu zài jiǎngshù yí duàn guānyú kōngqì liúdòng de gùshi. Chuāngtái shàng de huī zuì hòu, yīnwèi nàlǐ shì wàijiè wēilì jìnrù shìnèi de dì yī zhàn, xiàng hǎiguān yíyàng jiēnà le láizì chéngshì de wúshù lǚkè. Shūjià dǐngbù de huīchén zé chéngxiàn yì zhǒng jūnyún de huībáisè bómó, shuōmíng nàlǐ de kōngqì jīhū shì jìngzhǐ de, méiyǒu rénlèi huódòng de rǎodòng. Zuì yǒuqù de shì chúfáng chōu yóuyānjī shàngfāng de huīchén — tā dài zhe wēiwēi de huángsè, hùnhé le yóuzhī hé shuǐzhēngqì, xíngchéng le yì zhǒng niánxìng de chénjīwù, jìlù zhe měi yí dùn fàn de pēngrèn fāngshì. Wǒ yòng shǒuzhǐ zài bùtóng biǎomiàn qīngqīng huà guò, huīchén de zhìdì gè bù xiāngtóng: yǒu de gānzào shūsōng rú xì shā, yǒu de cháoshī miánmì rú nítǔ. Měi gé liǎng zhōu wǒ huì chèdǐ dǎsǎo yí cì, ránhòu guānchá huīchén chóngxīn jùjí de guòchéng. Tā zǒng shì xiān chūxiàn zài jiǎoluò hé fèngxì lǐ, fǎngfú nàxiē bèi yíwàng de kōngjiān duì wēilì yǒu yì zhǒng tèshū de yǐnlì. Huīchén shì chénmò de biānniánshǐ jiā, tā zhōngshí de jìlù zhe yí gè kōngjiān lǐ fāshēng de yíqiè — zhǐyào nǐ yuànyì fǔ xià shēn qù yuèdú.
text_en: After moving into the new apartment, I gradually learned to read dust. The dust in every corner tells a story about air flow. The dust on the windowsill is thickest, because that's the first stop for outside particles entering the room, accepting countless travelers from the city like a customs checkpoint. The dust atop the bookshelf presents an even grayish-white film, indicating the air there is nearly still, undisturbed by human activity. Most interesting is the dust above the kitchen range hood — it carries a slight yellow tint, mixed with grease and steam, forming a sticky deposit that records the cooking method of every meal. I run my finger lightly across different surfaces and the texture of dust varies: some dry and loose like fine sand, some moist and dense like earth. Every two weeks I do a thorough cleaning, then observe the process of dust regathering. It always appears first in corners and crevices, as if those forgotten spaces exert a special gravity on particles. Dust is a silent chronicler, faithfully recording everything that happens in a space — if only you're willing to bend down and read.
